prompt: Research how urban tree cover affects heat and public health in cities.

input.goal: urban tree cover heat public health cities
queries: urban tree cover heat island public health

result_preview.title: Urban trees reduce heat exposure
result_preview.snippet: Trees and vegetation reduce surface and air temperatures in cities.

result_preview.title: Urban tree cover and mortality
result_preview.snippet: Urban tree cover is associated with lower heat-related mortality.

result_preview.title: Urban greening and public health
result_preview.snippet: Urban green space can support public health and reduce heat stress.

result_preview.title: Using trees and vegetation to reduce heat islands
result_preview.snippet: Trees and vegetation reduce heat islands through shade and evapotranspiration.

result_preview.title: Urban tree cover and mortality
result_preview.snippet: Higher tree cover is associated with lower heat-related mortality.

result_preview.title: Urban green spaces and health
result_preview.snippet: Urban green space supports public health, heat resilience, and wellbeing.

runs.message: Urban tree cover reduces city heat through shade and evapotranspiration [1]. Research also links higher tree cover with lower heat-related mortality [2], while public-health guidance connects urban green space with heat resilience and wellbeing [3].

result_preview.title: Using trees and vegetation to reduce heat islands
result_preview.snippet: Trees and vegetation reduce city heat through shading and cooling.

result_preview.title: Heat and health
result_preview.snippet: Extreme heat can cause illness and death, with urban populations at risk.

result_preview.title: Urban green spaces and health

runs.message: The evidence points in the same direction: trees reduce urban heat exposure [1], and extreme heat is a direct public-health risk [2]. Urban green space is also associated with broader health and wellbeing benefits [3].
